title: Baremetrics vs ChartMogul vs Cheap Alternatives Under $50k MRR
meta_description: Honest breakdown of Baremetrics, ChartMogul, and lightweight alternatives for bootstrapped SaaS founders under $50k MRR. From someone who paid for both.
h1: Baremetrics vs ChartMogul vs the Cheap Stuff: What Actually Makes Sense Under $50k MRR
body_markdown:
I paid Baremetrics $129/mo for fourteen months while doing under $8k MRR. That's roughly $1,800 to learn that a pie chart of my MRR by plan didn't make me any money.

I'm writing this because every few weeks another founder in a Slack I'm in asks the same question: Baremetrics or ChartMogul, and is there something cheaper that doesn't suck? The answer has changed a lot since 2019, and most of the comparison posts you'll find are affiliate-stuffed garbage written by people who've never actually reconciled a failed Stripe webhook at 11pm.

So here's the honest version.

## The Baremetrics vs ChartMogul question is mostly the wrong question

Baremetrics and ChartMogul are basically the same product with different paint. Both connect to Stripe. Both calculate MRR, ARR, churn, LTV, and the usual subscription metrics. Both have public dashboards. Both will happily charge you more than your hosting bill.

The real differences, as of late 2024:

Baremetrics starts at $129/mo (the "Metrics" plan, rebranded from Startup) and scales up based on your tracked MRR. Once you cross $10k MRR you're looking at $179/mo. At $50k MRR you're paying $329/mo. They were acquired by a private equity-ish outfit called General Catalyst portfolio... actually, they were bought by an Earnest Capital-adjacent group in 2020 and the product roadmap has been noticeably quieter since. Recover (their dunning product) is still the best thing they ship.

ChartMogul has a free tier up to $10k MRR now — they added that in 2022 and it genuinely changed the calculus for early-stage founders. Above $10k MRR you're at $129/mo for their Launch plan, and it ramps from there. Their segmentation is better than Baremetrics'. Their UI feels like it was designed by people who use spreadsheets, which I mean as a compliment.

If I were choosing between just those two today, I'd pick ChartMogul. The free tier alone wins it. Baremetrics' dashboards look nicer in screenshots, but "looks nicer on Twitter" is not a line item I can expense.

But here's the thing I wish someone had told me in 2019: if you're doing under $50k MRR and you're a solo founder or a two-person team, you probably shouldn't be paying for either of them.

## What you actually look at vs what these tools show you

I ran an informal audit on myself for two months in 2022. Every time I opened Baremetrics, I wrote down what I actually looked at. Not what I *could* look at — what my eyes went to.

The list was embarrassingly short:

- Current MRR
- New MRR this month
- Churned MRR this month
- Net new customers
- Whether that big customer from last quarter was still active

That's it. Five numbers. I was paying $129/mo for five numbers I could pull from Stripe's own dashboard in about 90 seconds, or from a 50-line Python script in zero seconds after I wrote it once.

And the stuff Baremetrics and ChartMogul are actually *better* at — cohort analysis, LTV by segment, revenue forecasting — I wasn't mature enough as an operator to use. At $8k MRR, cohort analysis tells you almost nothing because your cohort sizes are 4 people. The confidence intervals are wider than the chart.

Jason Cohen wrote a post back in 2015 — I think it was on the WP Engine blog, might've been his personal blog — about how founders fetishize metrics that don't move decisions. That post has stuck with me. The question isn't "can I see this metric?" It's "what would I do differently if this number changed?"

For most sub-$50k MRR founders, the honest answer is: nothing. You'd keep shipping features and talking to customers regardless of whether your net revenue retention was 94% or 108%.

## The lightweight alternatives, ranked by how much I actually like them

There are maybe a dozen cheaper options now. I've tried most of them. Here's my take, in order:

**ProfitWell Metrics (now Paddle Metrics)** is free. It's been free since Paddle acquired ProfitWell in 2022 for around $200M. If you want the Baremetrics feature set at zero dollars, this is the answer, full stop. The catch: it's free because Paddle wants to upsell you on their payment infrastructure and retention products. The dashboard also feels neglected — Paddle clearly cares more about their billing platform than the free metrics tool. But for $0, you cannot complain.

**Equals** has a Stripe template that gives you a real spreadsheet you can actually modify. This is underrated. If you already think in spreadsheets (I do, after six years of running numbers in Google Sheets), Equals feels native. It's $99/mo for the team plan but there's a free tier that works for a single Stripe account.

**A custom dashboard you build in a weekend.** I'm serious. Stripe's API is well-documented. A Retool dashboard pulling from the Stripe API takes about four hours if you've done it before, eight if you haven't. I ran my business on exactly this for about nine months. It broke twice — once when Stripe deprecated an endpoint I was using, once when I forgot to handle proration on plan upgrades and my MRR number was off by about 6% for three weeks before I noticed. I got this wrong for six months, honestly. Don't build your own unless you're okay with that risk.

**Simple Analytics for Stripe, Outseta's built-in metrics, and the various "MRR tracker" tools on Product Hunt.** Most of these are fine. None of them are so much better than the others that I'd switch based on features alone. Pick one that has a founder who replies to emails.

One I'd avoid: anything that wants access to your Stripe account via API key rather than OAuth. This is table stakes in 2024 and if a tool is still asking for restricted API keys pasted into a form, they're either lazy or new. Either way, not my money.

## The actual question: what problem are you solving?

Here's the reframe I wish I'd done earlier.

There are three reasons founders buy MRR tools:

1. They want a number they can check quickly without logging into Stripe.
2. They want to understand *why* revenue is moving — cohort behavior, expansion, churn reasons.
3. They want to show the number to someone else (investors, a co-founder, Twitter).

If you're in bucket #1, you don't need Baremetrics or ChartMogul. You need a bookmark and a number. A cron job that posts MRR to your Slack every Monday morning solves this for free.

If you're in bucket #2, you probably need more customers before you need better analytics. I know that sounds dismissive. It's not meant to be. It's just that cohort analysis on 40 customers is astrology with better fonts.

If you're in bucket #3 — showing the number to others — then honestly, Baremetrics' public dashboards are still the gold standard. Buffer and ConvertKit famously ran theirs for years. But you're making a marketing decision, not an analytics decision, and you should price it accordingly.

> The tool doesn't make you disciplined about revenue. Looking at revenue every day makes you disciplined about revenue.

I stole that from someone on Twitter in 2021 and I can't find the original tweet. If it was yours, thank you.

## What I'd do if I were starting over at $2k MRR tomorrow

Use ProfitWell/Paddle Metrics for free until you hit about $15k MRR. By then you'll know whether you actually need deeper analytics or whether you just need a faster way to check the top-line number.

If it's the latter — and for most bootstrappers under $50k MRR, it is — get something narrower. A tool that does one thing well. I'll admit my bias here: I built **Stripe Revenue Tracker** partly because I wanted the five numbers I actually looked at, delivered to me without a login, without a $129 invoice, without a dashboard full of charts I'd never open. If that's not your problem, one of the options above will serve you fine.

The real lesson from my $1,800 Baremetrics bill isn't that Baremetrics is bad. It's a good product. The lesson is that I bought a Ferrari to drive to the mailbox. Match the tool to the job. At $8k MRR the job is: know the number, don't lose track, get back to shipping.
cover_image_prompt: Editorial illustration, dark mode, minimal concept art. Three abstract geometric dashboards floating at different elevations against a deep charcoal background — one large and ornate with concentric rings and intricate gauge patterns, one medium and balanced with clean rectangular panels, one small and stark with just a single glowing line. Subtle teal and muted amber accents. A faint grid recedes into the background. Slight paper grain texture. Premium tech magazine aesthetic, reminiscent of The Economist or Wired feature illustrations. Asymmetric composition, negative space dominant. No text, no people, no logos.
